modules.title: Embedded Liberalism & Economic Vulnerability

mechanisms.name: Embedded Liberalism Bargain
mechanisms.description: Trade openness → welfare compensation → political stability

mechanisms.name: Crisis of Compensation
mechanisms.description: Inability to provide effective compensation → isolationism and neonationalism

mechanisms.name: Unbundling Effect
mechanisms.description: Global value chains → individualized, unpredictable impacts → heightened insecurity

mechanisms.name: Regional Spillover
mechanisms.description: EGF assistance → regional economic stabilization → reduced populist appeal

mechanisms.name: Signaling Government Responsibility
mechanisms.description: Visible compensation → admission of costs → depoliticization of globalization

modules.core_argument: The historically embedded liberal bargain between trade openness and welfare protection has become strained by the inability of governments to effectively compensate globalization losers, creating a crisis of legitimacy as individual uncertainty increases and regional inequality persists.

modules.title: Automation & Technological Change

mechanisms.name: Routine Task Intensity (RTI) Effect
mechanisms.description: Task routineness/codifiability → automation susceptibility → political realignment

mechanisms.name: Anticipated vs. Experienced Hardship
mechanisms.description: Threat of job loss (not actual loss) → strongest radical-right support

mechanisms.name: Status Decline Through Demand Shift
mechanisms.description: Declining demand for routine work → status decline → social conservative politics

mechanisms.name: Occupational Downgrading Risk
mechanisms.description: Limited career mobility + declining demand → fear of economic decline

modules.core_argument: Automation vulnerability is determined by task routineness rather than skill levels, creating anticipated status decline (not immediate job loss) that drives support for right-wing populism among routine workers who remain employed.

modules.title: Globalization, Trade Exposure & Spatial Dimensions

mechanisms.name: China Shock Effect
mechanisms.description: Import competition → employment decline → persistent regional recession

mechanisms.name: Community Spillover
mechanisms.description: Individual economic outcomes linked by local economic conditions → collective political responses

mechanisms.name: Housing Market Lock-In
mechanisms.description: House prices → economic optimism/pessimism → political preferences → cultural ecosystems

mechanisms.name: Geographic Political Clustering
mechanisms.description: Regional decline → concentrated anti-system voting → place-based political realignment

mechanisms.name: Spatial Segregation Effect
mechanisms.description: Inequality → spatial distance → invisible poverty → meritocratic belief maintenance

modules.core_argument: Trade shocks create geographically concentrated, persistent regional decline that drives anti-system voting more strongly than individual-level economic factors, with housing markets structuring political geography through local optimism/pessimism cycles.

modules.title: Precarity, Skill Specificity & Occupational Risk

mechanisms.name: Skill Specificity Continuum
mechanisms.description: Specific skills → higher unemployment risk → greater vulnerability

mechanisms.name: Age-Differentiated Risk
mechanisms.description: Older workers: early retirement critical; younger workers: job opportunities critical

mechanisms.name: Multidimensional Precarity
mechanisms.description: Work conditions + tenure security + financial stability → combined populist effect

mechanisms.name: Anxious Meritocrat Effect
mechanisms.description: Meritocratic beliefs + economic anxiety → protectionist appeals

mechanisms.name: Downward Mobility Fear
mechanisms.description: Loss aversion → threat of falling in social ladder → political mobilization

modules.core_argument: Economic vulnerability varies continuously along skill specificity and occupational risk, with precarity showing distinct dimensions (work, tenure, financial) that affect political attitudes differently, and age cohorts requiring differentiated policy responses.

modules.title: Policy Feedback Effects & Welfare Institutions

mechanisms.name: Positive Inclusion Effects
mechanisms.description: Policies → inclusive experiences → broader political participation

mechanisms.name: Downward Spiral of Solidarity
mechanisms.description: Welfare cuts + conditionality → exclusion fears → harsh judgment of 'undeserving'

mechanisms.name: Upward Spiral of Solidarity
mechanisms.description: Social investment → inclusion → inclusive attitudes → universalistic solidarity

mechanisms.name: Self-Undermining Feedback
mechanisms.description: Intervention favoring out-groups → populist backlash → shift to radical right

mechanisms.name: Trust-Performance Loop
mechanisms.description: Poor performance → trust loss → worse evaluations → recursive decline

modules.core_argument: Welfare policies shape political attitudes through self-reinforcing or self-undermining feedback loops; policies create experiences that teach norms about deservingness, with enabling programs fostering upward spirals of solidarity while conditionality creates downward spirals.

modules.title: Active Labor Market Policies (ALMPs)

mechanisms.name: ALMP Typology Effect
mechanisms.description: Human capital investment + pro-market orientation → four distinct policy types

mechanisms.name: Implementation Quality
mechanisms.description: Funding stability + administrative capacity + tripartite dialogue → effectiveness

mechanisms.name: Enabling vs. Punitive
mechanisms.description: Enabling ALMPs → wellbeing gains; Punitive ALMPs → stigma and self-blame

mechanisms.name: Historical ALMP Shift
mechanisms.description: Post-war upskilling → 1970s-90s alternatives → 1990s+ weak investment focus

mechanisms.name: Employment Quality Effect
mechanisms.description: Wage subsidies/self-employment → wellbeing enhancement; Training → modest gains

modules.core_argument: ALMP effectiveness depends on type and implementation quality, with enabling programs emphasizing human capital development producing wellbeing gains while punitive programs create stigma; implementation dignity and choice are crucial.

modules.title: Welfare Design, Trust & Legitimacy

mechanisms.name: Procedural Fairness for Losers
mechanisms.description: Fair procedures matter more for dissatisfied citizens than for satisfied

mechanisms.name: Visibility-Priming Effect
mechanisms.description: Visible welfare policies → populist prime → increased opposition

mechanisms.name: CARIN Deservingness
mechanisms.description: Control + Attitude + Reciprocity + Identity + Need → deservingness judgment

mechanisms.name: Welfare Chauvinism
mechanisms.description: In-group preference + reciprocity criterion → citizen-only benefit access

mechanisms.name: Moral Communication
mechanisms.description: Respectful processes → human worth; Punitive processes → moral judgment of unworthiness

modules.core_argument: Welfare legitimacy depends on procedural fairness (especially for dissatisfied citizens), alignment with deservingness criteria, and moral messages conveyed through implementation; design communicates social worth independent of benefit levels.

modules.title: Status, Recognition & Relative Position

mechanisms.name: Status as Zero-Sum Game
mechanisms.description: Relative position matters; gains for some = losses for others

mechanisms.name: Recognition Over Redistribution
mechanisms.description: Status-anxious voters demand recognition/dignity rather than material compensation

mechanisms.name: Status Discordance Effect
mechanisms.description: Gap between expected/actual status → political dissatisfaction → radical voting

mechanisms.name: Status vs. Material Distinction
mechanisms.description: Status loss drives PRR support independent of material circumstances

mechanisms.name: Gender Differences
mechanisms.description: Status effects much weaker for women due to emancipatory experiences

mechanisms.name: Nostalgic Deprivation
mechanisms.description: Perceived status loss vs. past → radical right support

modules.core_argument: Economic status as relative position drives political behavior independently of material hardship; status-anxious voters demand recognition and dignity rather than redistribution, seeking to restore idealized past status.

modules.title: Ontological Security, Existential Anxiety & Psychology

mechanisms.name: Ontological Insecurity Threat
mechanisms.description: Rapid change → inability to maintain stable identity → existential anxiety

mechanisms.name: Anxiety as Political Mobilizer
mechanisms.description: Existential anxiety independent of attitudes → populist vulnerability

mechanisms.name: Globalization Securitization
mechanisms.description: Populists frame globalization as existential threat → mobilize anxiety

mechanisms.name: Fantasy Fulfillment
mechanisms.description: Impossible promises → emotional mobilization → blame redirection when unmet

mechanisms.name: Anger as Political Emotion
mechanisms.description: Anger → approach behavior + punishment of blamed groups

mechanisms.name: Life Dissatisfaction Effect
mechanisms.description: General life dissatisfaction → populist attitudes (stronger than economic concerns)

mechanisms.name: Loneliness Vulnerability
mechanisms.description: Loneliness → desire for community → populist mobilization

modules.core_argument: Rapid economic and social change threatens ontological security—the stable sense of self—creating existential anxiety that populist fantasies exploit by offering impossible promises of restoration and clarity.

modules.title: Moral Economy, Deservingness & Welfare Chauvinism

mechanisms.name: CARIN Deservingness Framework
mechanisms.description: Control + Attitude + Reciprocity + Identity + Need → welfare deservingness judgment

mechanisms.name: Producerism Moral Divide
mechanisms.description: Productive workers vs. parasitic strata above/below → welfare attitude formation

mechanisms.name: Welfare Chauvinism
mechanisms.description: Status anxiety + reciprocity criterion → in-group welfare preference

mechanisms.name: Defensive Othering
mechanisms.description: Welfare stigma → beneficiaries distance from 'less deserving' → reinforces negative narrative

mechanisms.name: Last-Place Aversion
mechanisms.description: Avoidance of bottom status → support for hierarchies toward lower groups

mechanisms.name: Kicking Down Mechanism
mechanisms.description: Exclusion experience → justify own deservingness by blaming lower groups

modules.core_argument: Moral frameworks (particularly producerism) and deservingness criteria shape welfare attitudes through moral boundary-drawing; status anxiety drives 'othering' where vulnerable populations distance themselves from even lower-status groups.

modules.title: Social Investment Paradigm

mechanisms.name: Capabilities Enhancement
mechanisms.description: Freedom + agency expansion → addresses both material needs and status concerns

mechanisms.name: Proactive vs. Reactive
mechanisms.description: Prevention of problems preferred over compensation for realized losses

mechanisms.name: Skill Investment Preference
mechanisms.description: Voters prefer skill investment as insurance vs. post-hoc compensation

mechanisms.name: Predistribution Preference
mechanisms.description: Less-educated prefer jobs/wages; more-educated prefer redistribution

mechanisms.name: Enabling ALMP Effects
mechanisms.description: Human capital development → wellbeing gains → inclusive solidarity

mechanisms.name: Dignity in Implementation
mechanisms.description: Respectful, autonomous programs → positive identity effects independent of benefits

mechanisms.name: Upward Spiral Creation
mechanisms.description: Social investment → inclusion → inclusive attitudes toward others

modules.core_argument: Social investment combining human capital development, capabilities enhancement, and dignity-affirming implementation can simultaneously address material needs, status concerns, and political legitimacy by creating upward spirals of inclusive solidarity.

modules.title: Populism, Welfare Chauvinism & Right-Wing Mobilization

mechanisms.name: Status Remedy Appeal
mechanisms.description: Populists offer restoration of idealized past status rather than material relief

mechanisms.name: Fantasy Mobilization
mechanisms.description: Impossible promises → emotional arousal → channeling desire for control

mechanisms.name: Securitization of Globalization
mechanisms.description: Framing globalization as existential threat → justifying radical action

mechanisms.name: In-Group/Out-Group Construction
mechanisms.description: Blameless ordinary people vs. culpable elites/threatening others

mechanisms.name: Group-Based Grievance Amplification
mechanisms.description: Individual losses → political when experienced as group loss

mechanisms.name: Sociotropic Status Decline
mechanisms.description: Community decline → individual status fears even without personal loss

mechanisms.name: Relative Outgroup Gains Resentment
mechanisms.description: Perceptions of outgroup gains → ingroup resentment → radical voting

mechanisms.name: Identity Switching
mechanisms.description: Economic shock + cultural contestation → class → cultural identity switch

mechanisms.name: Misattribution Effect
mechanisms.description: Automation → blamed on immigration/trade (cognitively available scapegoats)

modules.core_argument: Populist parties mobilize status-anxious and existentially insecure voters through fantasies of restoration and scapegoating, exploiting misattribution of structural problems to visible outgroups while offering impossible promises that deepen alienation when unfulfilled.

modules.title: Dual Pathway Synthesis & The Trilemma

mechanisms.name: Material Pathway
mechanisms.description: Economic hardship → demand for redistributive policies

mechanisms.name: Status Pathway
mechanisms.description: Status threats → demand for status-affirming policies

mechanisms.name: Temporal Pathway
mechanisms.description: Anticipated hardship (fear) vs. experienced hardship → different responses

mechanisms.name: Institutional Pathway
mechanisms.description: Welfare design → deservingness perceptions → solidarity norms → political attitudes

mechanisms.name: Competing Policy Demands
mechanisms.description: Material vs. status pathways → different (sometimes conflicting) demands

mechanisms.name: Welfare-Status-Democracy Trilemma
mechanisms.description: Material adequacy + Status recognition + Political sustainability → no traditional solution

mechanisms.name: Breaking Recursive Cycles
mechanisms.description: Policy design aligning with beliefs/experiences → reduced polarization

mechanisms.name: Social Investment Solution
mechanisms.description: SI addresses all three trilemma dimensions simultaneously

modules.core_argument: Economic vulnerability operates through four interconnected pathways (material, status, temporal, institutional) that create a trilemma: traditional policies cannot simultaneously satisfy material needs, status concerns, and political legitimacy; social investment offers potential resolution.

modules.title: Cognitive Frames, Belief Systems & Political Realignment
modules.h1: Cognitive Frames, Belief Systems & Political Realignment

mechanisms.name: Misattribution Mechanism
mechanisms.description: Automation anxiety → blamed on immigration/trade via cognitive availability, political entrepreneurship, and psychological gratification

mechanisms.name: Identity Switching
mechanisms.description: Economic shock + cultural contestation → switch from class to cultural identity → reduced redistribution demand, increased cultural politics

mechanisms.name: Social Insulation Loop
mechanisms.description: Inequality → spatial segregation → limited cross-class exposure → meritocratic beliefs → inequality legitimation → more inequality

mechanisms.name: Anxious Meritocrat Effect
mechanisms.description: Meritocratic beliefs + economic anxiety → trade framed as 'unfair' rule-breaking → protectionist support

mechanisms.name: Universalism-Particularism Cleavage
mechanisms.description: Moral orientation (equal treatment vs. in-group loyalty) → organizes inequality attitudes and welfare preferences across ideological spectrum

mechanisms.name: Affective Polarization
mechanisms.description: Partisan identity moralization → out-party aversion → democratic attitude erosion (via othering and moralization dimensions)

mechanisms.name: Representation Gap Effect
mechanisms.description: Policy discontent + issue salience → populist attitudes; class gradient strongest for pensions and unemployment

mechanisms.name: Policy-as-Signal
mechanisms.description: Fiscal cutbacks signal government priority incompatibility → vulnerable voter accountability withdrawal

modules.core_argument: Political responses to economic vulnerability are cognitively mediated by misattribution (blaming automation on immigration), identity switching (class to cultural identity), and moral belief systems (meritocracy, universalism/particularism), while spatial segregation legitimizes inequality by limiting its visibility; representation gaps trigger populist attitudes when salient policies are seen as unaddressed.

modules.title: Mechanisms Catalog

mechanisms.name: Economic Grievance Activation
mechanisms.description: Economic hardship → trigger for political responses

mechanisms.name: Policy Feedback (Self-Reinforcing)
mechanisms.description: Policies create supportive constituencies

mechanisms.name: Policy Feedback (Self-Undermining)
mechanisms.description: Policies generate opposition to themselves

mechanisms.name: Status as Zero-Sum
mechanisms.description: Relative gains/losses drive political behavior

mechanisms.name: Identity Switching
mechanisms.description: Class → cultural identity under economic shock + cultural contestation

mechanisms.name: Ontological Insecurity
mechanisms.description: Rapid change threatens stable identity → anxiety

mechanisms.name: Procedural Fairness
mechanisms.description: Fair processes matter more for dissatisfied citizens

mechanisms.name: ALMP Type Effects
mechanisms.description: Enabling ALMPs → wellbeing gains; Punitive → stigma

modules.core_argument: Comprehensive catalog of causal mechanisms linking economic vulnerability, welfare institutions, and political behavior, organized by category for systematic analysis.

key_theoretical_frameworks: Social Investment Paradigm (Nicaise, 2021; Hemerijck et al., 2023)

usage_guide.for_comprehensive_understanding: Read 13 (Dual Pathway Synthesis) then refer to specific modules
